s2.q: Which of the following BEST defines primary postpartum hemorrhage (PPH)?
options: Blood loss >500 mL within the first 24 hours after delivery
Blood loss >1000 mL within the first 24 hours after delivery
Blood loss >500 mL from 24 hours to 6 weeks postpartum
Blood loss >1000 mL regardless of timing
s2.explain: Primary PPH is defined as blood loss >500 mL within the first 24 hours after delivery.

s2.q: The most common cause of primary postpartum hemorrhage is:
s2.explain: Uterine atony accounts for approximately 70-80% of primary PPH cases.

s2.q: Which statement about PPH types is TRUE?
options: Primary PPH occurs after 24 hours up to 6 weeks
Secondary PPH occurs within the first 24 hours
Primary PPH occurs within the first 24 hours after delivery
Secondary PPH is always due to trauma
s2.explain: Primary PPH = first 24 hours; Secondary PPH = after 24 hours up to 6 weeks.

s2.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s2.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s2.q: The incidence of this condition is highest in which population?
options: Depends on the specific condition and risk factors
s2.explain: Incidence varies by condition — each has its own epidemiology and risk profile.

s3.q: The '4 T's' of primary PPH etiology include all EXCEPT:
s3.explain: The 4 T's are: Tone (atony), Trauma, Tissue (retained), and Thrombin (coagulopathy).

s3.q: Which of the following is a risk factor for uterine atony?
s3.explain: All are risk factors. Overdistension (twins/polyhydramnios), prolonged labor, and placental abnormalities all predispose to atony.

s3.q: The most common type of PPH is due to:
s3.explain: Atonic PPH (uterine atony) is the single most common cause, accounting for ~80% of primary PPH.

s3.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s3.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s3.q: The etiology of this condition involves:
options: A complex interplay of genetic, environmental, and multifactorial elements
s3.explain: Most obstetric conditions have multifactorial etiology.

s4.q: The fastest bedside differentiator between atonic and traumatic PPH is:
options: Color of the blood (dark vs bright red)
s4.explain: A soft, boggy uterus = atony; a firm, contracted uterus with ongoing bleeding = trauma until proven otherwise.

s4.q: Which of the following is a preventive measure for PPH during labor?
options: Routine use of uterotonics (oxytocin) after delivery of the baby
Exploration of the birth canal after delivery
Careful observation in the 4th stage of labor
s4.explain: Routine uterotonics after delivery of the baby is the key intrapartum preventive measure. The other options are also valid but belong to different phases.

s4.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s4.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.

s4.q: The gold standard diagnostic test is:
s4.explain: The best diagnostic method varies by condition — some rely on imaging, others on lab tests or clinical findings.

s4.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s4.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s5.q: Which uterotonic is contraindicated in a woman with preeclampsia and PPH?
s5.explain: Ergot alkaloids (methylergonovine/ergometrine) are contraindicated in hypertension/preeclampsia as they raise blood pressure. Safe options: oxytocin and misoprostol.

s5.q: What is the FIRST step in managing atonic PPH?
s5.explain: Uterine massage is the first-line mechanical treatment. The stepwise approach is: massage → aortic compression if severe → medications → surgery/balloon.

s5.q: Oxytocin is considered first-line medical treatment for PPH. Its recommended dose is:
s5.explain: Oxytocin dose: 10-40 IU IM or IV drip. Options A, C, D correspond to ergometrine, carboprost, and misoprostol respectively.

options: Depends on the specific condition and severity
s5.explain: Management approach varies — some conditions require surgery, others medical or conservative care.

s5.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s5.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

s6.q: Secondary postpartum hemorrhage is defined as bleeding after the first 24 hours up to:
s6.explain: Secondary PPH is bleeding after 24 hours until 6 weeks (end of puerperium). Its etiology is fundamentally different: infection + retention.

s6.q: Which of the following is a late complication of massive PPH?
s6.explain: Sheehan syndrome = ischemic pituitary infarction following massive hemorrhage, leading to panhypopituitarism. The others are early/management-related complications.
Fetal well-being is closely linked to maternal condition — multiple fetal complications are possible.

s6.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s6.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s6.q: In obstetric practice, this knowledge helps:
s6.explain: Comprehensive knowledge enables prevention, early diagnosis, and optimal management.

options: Prolapse of the uterus into the vagina
Collapse of the fundus into the uterine cavity — the uterus is turned inside out
Rupture of the lower uterine segment
s7.explain: Uterine inversion: the fundus collapses into the uterine cavity, turning the uterus inside outward.

s7.q: The MOST common cause of uterine inversion is:
options: Iatrogenic — excessive cord traction with fundal placental attachment
s7.explain: Iatrogenic inversion (most common) from excessive cord traction with a fundal placenta or fundal pressure on a relaxed uterus.

s7.q: In 1st degree uterine inversion, the fundus is:
options: Protruding to or beyond the introitus
s7.explain: 1st degree (incomplete) = fundus depressed within endometrial cavity; 2nd = through cervical os; 3rd = to/beyond introitus.

s7.q: When assessing this aspect, the most important factor is:
s7.explain: Assessment requires integration of clinical, laboratory, and imaging findings.

s7.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s7.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s8.q: Retained placenta is defined as failure of the placenta to spontaneously separate within:
options: 15 minutes after delivery of the fetus
30 minutes after delivery of the fetus
1 hour after delivery of the fetus
Immediately after delivery of the fetus
s8.explain: Retained placenta = placenta fails to separate within 30 minutes after delivery of the fetus. It is the second leading cause of PPH.

s8.q: In placenta accreta, the chorionic villi penetrate:
options: The superficial layer of the myometrium
The deep layer of the myometrium
s8.explain: P. accreta = villi penetrate superficial myometrium; P. increta = deep myometrium; P. percreta = up to peritoneal coat.

s8.q: Which of the following is a cause of postpartum collapse?
s8.explain: Postpartum collapse has 8 causes including anesthetic complications, MI, pulmonary embolism, AFE, rupture uterus, eclampsia, massive hemorrhage, and sepsis.

s8.q: Which of the following is a key concept?
options: It is essential for understanding the condition
It is rarely relevant in clinical practice
It applies only to specific populations
It has been replaced by newer classifications
s8.explain: Understanding key concepts is fundamental to clinical management.

s8.q: The clinical significance of this section is:
options: It applies only to research settings
s8.explain: Understanding these concepts directly informs clinical management.
